original_abstract:
We investigated the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-isoprene epoxydiols (IEPOX) onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of added strong acids. Our results show that while no organic aerosol (OA) formation occurs on dry ammonium sulfate (AS) seeds, OA growth is both prompt and efficient on AS seeds with a liquid water content (LWC) ranging from 40% to 75% of the total particle mass. This OA formation is a kinetically limited process, meaning growth continues as long as a gas-phase reservoir of IEPOX exists. We found no significant differences in OA yield or chemical composition between the two isomers.

Notably, reactive uptake onto hydrated AS seeds treated with a base (NaOH) still resulted in high OA loadings, indicating that the pH dependence of IEPOX-derived OA formation on ammonium-containing particles is relatively weak. In contrast, no OA mass remained after drying when sodium (Na+) was substituted for ammonium (NH4+) in the seed particles, highlighting the critical role of the ammonium ion. We determined the Henry’s Law partitioning coefficient of IEPOX on NaCl particles (ionic strength ~9 M) to be 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (−50 / +100%). Replacing the sulfate (SO₄²⁻) anion with chloride (Cl⁻) in NH4+-based seeds significantly reduced OA production, likely due to differences in nucleophilic strength. 

Analysis via online aerosol mass spectrometry and offline filters revealed a complex organic composition including oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates, and, for the first time, amines. These findings help explain the weak correlations between IEPOX-derived OA, particle acidity, and liquid water observed in field studies; in these humid, ammonium-rich environments, the chemical system is limited by the availability of specific nucleophiles rather than catalyst activity or water volume.
humanized_abstract:
This study examines the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed surfaces in the absence of additional strong acid. Under the conditions examined, no OA forms on dry ammonium sulfate (AS) seed, but is quickly and strongly produced on AS seeds with relatively moderate liquid water content between 40–75% (w/w). This appears to be a kinetically limited process as growth to maximum value was observed for as long as IEPOX was available in the gas phase. Finally, there were essentially no differences between the cis- and trans-IEPOX isomers in terms of total yield, composition or retention in the particle phase.

Although reactive uptake onto hydrated AS seeds treated with NaOH led to substantial OA loadings, these levels were not highly dependent on pH, suggesting that the pH dependence for IEPOX-derived OA formed on ammonium-containing particles is not high. Upon drying, there was little remaining OA mass when sodium was substituted for ammonium in the seed particles. The Henry’s Law coefficient of IEPOX adsorbed onto NaCl particles at an ionic strength of 9 M (adjusted to match conditions of seeds made from 10 wt % ammonium sulfate solution in 50 mM H2SO4) was found to be 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (range of −50 / +100%). Replacing sulfate with chloride in the seeds of ammonium sulfate particles had a pronounced effect on OA yields.

Online aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS) and offline filter analysis were used to identify complex organic aerosol (OA) composition over rural/forested areas, which included oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates, and other lesser identified species, including amines. Understanding these observations can help explain the observations of low correlation between IEPOX-derived OA and particle acidity/liquid water in humid/ammonium-rich conditions; specifically that this system is limited by availability of particular nucleophiles rather than catalyst activity or availability of water.

document.input:
We investigated the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-isoprene epoxydiols (IEPOX) onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of added strong acids.
